title: C# List Collections
content:
### List<T> in C#

`List<T>` is a dynamic array that can grow and shrink automatically. It is the most commonly used collection in C#.

### Creating Lists

```csharp
List<int> numbers = new List<int>();
List<string> names = new List<string>() { "Alice", "Bob", "Charlie" };
var scores = new List<double> { 85.5, 92.3, 78.0 };
```

### Adding Elements

```csharp
List<int> list = new List<int>();
list.Add(10);              // Add single
list.AddRange(new[] { 20, 30, 40 });  // Add multiple
list.Insert(1, 15);         // Insert at index
list.InsertRange(2, new[] { 25, 35 });  // Insert multiple
```

### Removing Elements

```csharp
list.Remove(10);            // Remove first occurrence
list.RemoveAt(0);           // Remove at index
list.RemoveAll(x => x > 30);  // Remove by condition
list.RemoveRange(1, 3);     // Remove range
list.Clear();               // Remove all
```

### Accessing Elements

```csharp
List<string> fruits = new() { "Apple", "Banana", "Cherry" };

string first = fruits[0];        // Index (zero-based)
string last = fruits[^1];        // Index from end (C# 8+)
string fruit = fruits[^2];       // Second from end

// Safe access with bounds checking
if (fruits.Count > 0) { }
```

### Common Methods

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `Add()` | Add element to end |
| `AddRange()` | Add multiple elements |
| `Insert()` | Insert at index |
| `Remove()` | Remove first match |
| `RemoveAt()` | Remove at index |
| `IndexOf()` | Find index of element |
| `Contains()` | Check if element exists |
| `Sort()` | Sort the list |
| `Reverse()` | Reverse order |
| `Find()` | Find first match |
| `FindAll()` | Find all matches |
| `Exists()` | Check if any match |
| `TrueForAll()` | Check all match condition |

### Searching and Finding

```csharp
List<int> nums = new() { 5, 3, 8, 1, 9, 3 };

int firstMatch = nums.Find(x => x > 5);       // 8
List<int> allMatch = nums.FindAll(x => x > 3);  // 5, 8, 9
int index = nums.IndexOf(3);                   // 1
int lastIndex = nums.LastIndexOf(3);           // 5
bool exists = nums.Exists(x => x > 10);        // false
bool allPositive = nums.TrueForAll(x => x > 0); // true
```

### List Capacity

```csharp
List<int> list = new List<int>(100);  // Initial capacity
Console.WriteLine($"Capacity: {list.Capacity}");  // 100
Console.WriteLine($"Count: {list.Count}");  // 0

// Trim excess
list.TrimExcess();
```

> **Key Point:** List<T> is the go-to collection for most scenarios. It provides O(1) index access and O(n) search. Use `Capacity` to pre-allocate memory for large lists. `Count` returns the actual number of elements.

### Converting to Array

```csharp
int[] arr = list.ToArray();
```

### List Initialization Syntax

```csharp
var numbers = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 };
// Collection expressions (C# 12+)
List<int> numbers2 = [1, 2, 3];
int[] array = [1, 2, 3];
```
